titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Étienne Dumont, July 9, 1811
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

respStmt.orgName: Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of the Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Étienne Dumont, July 9, 1811

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

opener.dateline: Do not shew this letter to any body) Edgeworth Town52 July 9th . 1811.

div.p:
When you wrote to me on the 4th . I
think you had not received a joint letter
from my father and me, written I believe
about the 24th . of last month & sent under
cover to Lord Lansdowne . It has proba-
blyprobably reached you by this time, and you
may, from the eagerness of our hopes, &
urgency of our invitation form some
idea of the disappointment I felt this
morning on opening your letter, and find-
ingfinding that your promised visit is again de-
layeddelayed - my father's share of the disappoint
mentdisappointment , poor man!, is yet to come - He returns
from Dublin to day, & the disagreeable
news with which I have to greet him is that
M. Dumont is detained another month
in London ! - If your nephew were an an-
gelangel I should bear him malice at this
moment & the only way by which he can
ever make me amends for the pain he has
given me is by coming along with bringing you to
us. You see I am obstinate as well as your-
selfyourself, but when I have so good a cause I call
this - resolution - not obstinacy -

You urge us to come to London -
Now . . before my father returns home and
when I write from myself, and not as his
secretary, I am determined to tell you my
mind upon that subject; and you who have
such perfect tact will immediately perceive
that I tell you the truth - and the whole truth.

If this whole large family consisting of
5 children of Mrs . E's , from 12 to 1 year old
- A sister of 18 and two elderly good aunts
who live with us, could all be transported
and established in a goodhouse in London
and if our living there for a winter did
not interfere with the establishing my
brother William (a most promising young
man of 17 in his profession of a civil engi-
neerengineer ) - I should like to spend a winter in
London very much-

But - these ifs are absolute impossi-
53 bilitiesimpossibilities - William's advancement in his profes-
sionprofession depends upon his remaining in Ireland
at present where he has employment at
in surveying these bogs & his being employed depends
upon my father's name & credit being joined
with his - William out of the question, -
our fortune is not sufficient to make a
journey for so large a family, & the taking
a large house in London &c prudent - My
father and Mrs . Edgeworth would I know
do much to procure for me any amuse-
mentamusement , or gratification of any kind - much
more than I could either desire or would
accept - - But why could not a part of the
family you will ask come to London? - I
will tell you - Because Mrs . Edgeworth would
could not leave her children without great re-
gretregret , and inconvenience nor without &putting herself
under obligations to those who should
take care of them in her absence - and I , know
ingknowing all this, could not be happy in letting
her make such a sacrifice for me - Nor should
I be at all happy in going as she has often pro
-posedproposed that I should, with my father without
her - You will understand that though she
is my mother-in law she is, next to my fa-
therfather , the friend I love best in the world & I shd
not enjoy any pleasure without her - on the
contrary I should feel pain & continual anxiety
from taking my father away from her, which
would absolutely prevent all possibility of my
being happy or even amused in London or any
whereanywhere else - Now ... trusting still to your pe-
netrationpenetration , that you will not suspect me of
affectation of sentiment or affectation of humility, I will
venture to tell you more of my mind - more
than I would tell to anyone who I thought
considered me only as an authoress - more
than I would tell to any frenchman because
I know, with the best dispositions possible to
believe me, he could not - But you are a Swiss &
are a friend, & therefore I trust you can & will
believe me, when I tell you, that, what the French
call un grand succes in society would add
very little to my happiness - I have tried Paris
& Edinburgh & Dublin and know pretty well
what the pleasures of seeing & hearing & being
seen & heard amount to; and I enjoy amuse
-mentamusement & compliments & flattery all in their
just proportion - But they are as 0 in my
scale, compared with domestic life - And
whenever I have been in a capital city -(no
later for instance than last winter, so I have a
recollection sufficiently clear to be sure certain
that I am not mistaken)- I have always looked
through the crowd of acquaintances to see whe-
54 therwhether there was a possibility of making a friend out of any of
them - all the rest pass as away like the figures in
a panorama - But I have always felt deep regretted deep
-lydeeply when I have transiently seen, & been obliged to
part from any who might on further acquaintance
have become friends - And this is one of the coils
of a town life - people are doomed to meet in
such crowds or with so many circumstances
of constraint or theatric decoration, that they can
never not in ten years become as well acquaint
edacquainted with each others opinions & characters as they
could in ten days, or ten hours in a private
family in the country - Common characters
such as compose & must ever compose the great-
-ergreater part of general society, pass away from my
mind without leaving the slightest trace
behind - I take what amusement I can from
them, when I am in their company, & am said
to be always very civil to them, but they would
detest me, if they saw into my mind & knew
how completely indifferent they all are to me,
& what a waste of time I think all that I spend
with them - As to the good it might do me
as a writer to see the fashionables of London I do
not think from my own experience that I
should expect nearly the advantage from
it that you do - I do not think I was much - if at
at all improved - as a writer, by a writer
in Paris, or by a visit to Edinburgh or Dublin - The
views of characters in society are too confined &
transitory - There is not time to see justly -Traveller
-& the objects are too near - Travellers seldom from
such cursory observation represent manners
much less motives faithfully - I have, I think, gained
more as a writer by hearing & comparing the re
presentationsrepresentations of persons of sense & of fools who have
furnished me with facts & observations accord-
ingaccording to their various ways of seeing and feeling
-Besides I have a father & brothers & friends
who continually supply me with variety from
that world in which I don't live, or wish to live -
-If I were merely a writer I might perhaps see
better & see only as a writer in society - but a number
of different feelings - many of them most trifling
& foolish perhaps,- some for the friends who are
with me,- some for myself disturb my spirit
of observation, & unfit me for a philosophical
spectatress in the world - For instance I might at
a dinner with the grandest or wittiest people
in London be totally absorbed in considering
whether the bones in the fish my father was eat-
ingeating would choakchoke him or not, or (more foolish still
-whether my mothers cap became her, or
whether the persons who might be compliment
ingcomplimenting me thought me ideotidiot or authoress enough to
believe them au pied de la lettre -

I am ashamed to see that I have written so much
about myself - but, paradoxical as it may seem,
I have done so, from an anxious desire to see
& know more of you - I have done so, to convince
you that there is little or no chance of our seeing
you in London - If you were to send me the
"rouleau de signatures" you mention, I do not
know any name public or private that would
have so much weight with me as that of Dumont -
And I would much rather see him at Edge-
worthEdgeworth town than in London for a thousand reasons,
chiefly, because I should see more of him, and
55 (Oh! pretender to humility & monster of pride!-)
because he would see and know more of me than
he possibly could in a capital city - If I were at
this instant asked

Would you rather spend next winter in
London or that M. Dumont should spend two
months at Edgeworth Town ?

I should without the slightest hesi-
tationhesitation answer - that M. Dumont should spend
two months at Edgeworth Town - I feel convinc
-edconvinced that our manner of living- & I will boldly
say at once, our whole family would be suited
to your taste, and that the more you knew
of my father & Mrs . E the more you would
love & esteem them - in short I am certain
could by that visit make an intimate
of the person to whom we now all look up as
the superior person of this day -

As to Bentham - I have not patience
with him - I think he uses you extremely ill
- Will you send for me the enclosed note
to Johnsons to desire him to send us your
book as soon as possible - Accept our most
grateful thanks for your kindness in giving
us your first copy -

Of PschychePsyche or of Madame de Stael , or even her
German letters I cannot think at present
